did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a three-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with backpropagation to classify morphologic changes in pigmented skin lesions. The ANN takes features (diameter ratio, compactness, eccentricity) from segmented macroscopic images as input and outputs a risk indicator for lesion changes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original computer-aided evaluation system for tracking morphologic changes in pigmented skin lesions using a three-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network. It details the methodology, including feature extraction (diameter ratio, compactness, eccentricity) and ANN architecture, and reports experimental validation results on a dataset of 200 macroscopic lesion images, achieving an average accuracy of 82%.

title: Computer Aided Evaluation (CAE) of Morphologic Changes in Pigmented Skin Lesions
brief_summary: This study presents a computer-aided evaluation system for tracking morphologic changes in pigmented skin lesions to support early melanoma detection. The method utilizes a three-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with backpropagation to classify changes based on features like diameter ratio, compactness, and eccentricity extracted from segmented macroscopic images. The system was validated using a dataset of 200 macroscopic lesion images (100 pairs) sourced from various dermatology centers. Evaluation was performed by comparing the ANN's output against the ground truth opinions of three dermatologists. The proposed method achieved an average accuracy of 82% in detecting lesions with appreciable morphologic changes, with optimal performance observed using 7 neurons in the hidden layer. The authors conclude that the system is suitable for telemedicine and smartphone-based self-examination due to its use of standard camera images and computational efficiency.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on tracking morphologic changes in pigmented skin lesions (moles) to support early detection of melanoma. The dataset consists of 200 macroscopic images (100 pairs) of these lesions. While melanoma is mentioned as the most common skin cancer and the context for early detection, the specific classification task is binary: detecting 'appreciable changes' vs 'no appreciable changes' in the morphology of the pigmented lesions, rather than classifying specific histological types like BCC or SCC.

Melanoma is cited as the most common skin cancer and the primary motivation for early detection via mole tracking. It is not a direct label in the classification task, which focuses on morphological change detection in pigmented lesions generally.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of morphologic changes in pigmented skin lesions (appreciable changes vs. no appreciable changes).
all_task_types.details: The paper employs a three-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network (ANN) to classify whether a pigmented skin lesion has undergone appreciable morphologic changes. The target output is binary: '1 for appreciable changes and 0 for no appreciable changes'. The model uses features (diameter ratio, compactness, eccentricity) extracted from segmented macroscopic images as input. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (82%) against ground truth opinions of dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: Dermatology Center Database
datasets_used.description: A collection of macroscopic lesion images acquired with standard cameras, sourced from the databases of various local dermatology centers.

use.training: 80 images (40 pairs) were used for network training.
use.testing: 120 images (60 pairs) were used as the test bench for method evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to measure error during the training phase of the Artificial Neural Network to minimize differences between output values and target values.
Used to evaluate the performance of the method in detecting lesions with appreciable morphologic changes, compared against the ground truth opinions of three dermatologists.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (82%) and mean square error, comparing the ANN output against the ground truth opinions of three dermatologists.

Dermatology Center Database.dataset_description: 200 macroscopic lesion images (100 pairs) from various local dermatology centers

architecture_details.activation_function: Not explicitly named, but standard ANN described

features_used: Ratio of lesion maximum diameter to minimum diameter

performance.accuracy_description: Average accuracy in detecting lesions for which morphologic changes warrant particular attention

best_configuration.note: Best performance obtained with 7 neurons in the hidden layer

performance.ground_truth_comparison: Compared against opinions of three different dermatologists

Dermatology Center Database.notes: The method achieves an average accuracy value of about 82%. The best performance is obtained adopting an ANN classifier with 7 neurons in the hidden layer. The training phase is terminated when 500 iterations have been performed.

architecture_used.input_size: 6 neurons (input vector formed by twelve feature values, six for each of the two segmented images)

architecture_details.hidden_layer_neurons: Varied between 5 and 13; best performance with 7 neurons
architecture_details.activation_function: Not explicitly named (described as nonlinear characteristic mapped by activation function)

classification_or_output_head.output_description: Risk indicator (1 for appreciable changes, 0 for no appreciable changes)

preprocessing_techniques: Image segmentation (method not specified, described as adaptable to any literature method)
Border detection (method not specified, described as adaptable to any literature method)
Feature extraction: calculation of diameter ratio (maximum to minimum diameter), lesion compactness (ratio of squared perimeter to area), and lesion eccentricity (related to ellipsoid hull)
conclusions: The proposed computer-aided evaluation system effectively tracks morphologic changes in pigmented skin lesions using macroscopic images captured with standard cameras.
The system achieved an average accuracy of 82% in detecting lesions with appreciable morphologic changes when validated against the ground truth opinions of three dermatologists.
Optimal performance was obtained using a three-layer feed-forward Artificial Neural Network with 7 neurons in the hidden layer.
The method is independent of the specific image segmentation or border detection procedure used, allowing it to serve as the final stage of a mole tracking system.

Due to its simplicity and use of standard camera inputs, the system is particularly suitable for telemedicine applications and implementation on smartphones for patient self-examination and primary care physician support.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, non-public dataset of only 200 macroscopic lesion images (100 pairs) sourced from various local dermatology centers, limiting generalizability.
The ground truth for evaluation is based on the subjective opinions of three dermatologists rather than histopathological confirmation or a standardized clinical consensus protocol.
The paper does not report external validation; the method was only tested on the internal split of the Dermatology Center Database.
No statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) are provided for the reported 82% accuracy, making it difficult to assess the robustness of the performance.

The classification task is binary (appreciable changes vs. no appreciable changes) and does not distinguish between specific lesion types (e.g., melanoma vs. benign nevi), limiting direct clinical diagnostic utility.
The system depends on prior image segmentation and border detection steps which are not detailed in the paper, introducing potential variability based on the upstream segmentation method.
The training phase was fixed at 500 iterations without reporting convergence analysis or early stopping criteria, which may affect model optimization.
The dataset composition (e.g., distribution of lesion types, skin tones, or image quality) is not described, raising concerns about potential population or dataset bias.
